_source_of_truth_note: Canonical scenario spec for Wasteland Crystals. Per the operative narrative frame (proposals/PROPOSAL-WASTELAND-CRYSTALS.md §'Update 2026-05-07: Operative narrative frame — mining operation'): the player is a future landfill miner working a cell, not an explorer or curator. Each scenario IS a real ELFM (Enhanced Landfill Mining) site characterization, with phase identifications drawn from peer-reviewed excavation papers wherever possible. The expected_species map links scenarios to mineral catalog entries via evidence_role, distinguishing what the cited paper actually observed from what the chemistry implies but the paper didn't name.

_schema.name: Display name. Lead with the locality — surveyor's voice.
_schema.site_class: Site classification: municipal_landfill | mswi_bottom_ash_cell | mixed_msw_legacy | leachate_collection_biofilm | construction_demolition_pile.
_schema.location: {country, region, locality} — populated for real sites, partial for archetypal.
_schema.operator_or_excavator: Who pulled the cores or pipes. NEW-MINE consortium for the European ELFM corpus.
_schema.excavation_year: Year the characterization study was performed (not when the cell was filled).
_schema.citations: Primary characterization papers. Use the same short-form 'Author Year Journal Vol:Page' convention as minerals.json.
_schema.evidence_level: Parallel to mineral catalog: landfill_specific (peer-reviewed characterization of this exact site) | program_synthesis (this site contributed to a multi-site review whose findings are reported jointly) | chemistry_archetype (no real-site characterization; the scenario is an archetypal recipe).
_schema.waste_age_years: Approximate age of the waste at characterization time. Drives the dominant_phase default.
_schema.dominant_phase: Leachate phase governing the cell at characterization time: acid (yrs 1-10) | methanogenic (decades) | stable (centuries) | alkaline_cap (cement-dominated) | burn_zone (post-incineration). Multiple lifts in a real cell live in different phases simultaneously; this is the reported governing phase for the characterization horizon.
_schema.substrate_inventory: Array of substrate-token entries with {token, evidence_basis, note}. evidence_basis: 'documented' (the cited paper specifically names this substrate in this cell) | 'implied_typical_msw' (a typical MSW-cell component the citation paper did not specifically characterize but whose presence is implied by the cell type and waste-stream composition). Narrators (js/04-narrators.ts) compose paragenesis from precursors whose source_substrate appears here, hedging language for implied_typical_msw entries so the prose stays honest about evidence pedigree.
_schema.leachate_chemistry: Characteristic ion concentrations for the dominant_phase. Values typical of the phase rather than direct measurement — only Pb/PO4/Cl/Zn/S2 are reported with site-specific pedigree where the citation supports it. Honest about evidence.
_schema.expected_species: Map from mineral catalog ID to {evidence_role, note}. evidence_role: directly_observed (XRD/SEM identification in the cited paper) | implied_by_substrate_chemistry (the relics + leachate make the phase chemically necessary, but the paper does not name it) | predicted_from_program_synthesis (the phase appears in a related ELFM review covering this site without being named in a site-specific paper).
_schema.active_zones: Subset of zone IDs (from data/zones.json) currently dominant or running active chemistry at this scenario's snapshot. The renderer tints only these in the schematic. NOTE: this is a current-chemistry filter, not a crystal-presence filter — crystals nucleated in any historically-active zone persist even after the zone has waned, so the crystal-dot generator places dots based on the mineral's expected zones in zones.json regardless of active_zones state. The two systems are complementary: active_zones = current chemistry territory; crystal dots = accumulated paragenesis since cell closure.
_schema.items_in_cell: Map from item_class_id (data/item_classes.json) to representative count of that item type in the cell. Counts are RENDER counts — a few-dozen visible items per cell, not the actual million-plus item count of a real landfill. Procedurally placed by js/07-cell-population.ts with a deterministic seed (scenario.id + class_id + item_index). Per the boss's 2026-05-07 framing: 'track what garbage is where before it breaks down.' Items pre-exist chemistry; their substrate tokens contribute to local chemistry and (in future passes) gate which crystals nucleate where.
_schema.events: Optional array of fire / smoldering events that have affected this cell. Per proposals/HANDOFF-BURN-ZONE.md: events are the fourth axis (zone × substrate × chemistry × event), non-equilibrium overlays that compose with steady-state chemistry rather than replacing it. Schema: {id, type, ignition_step, current_status, spatial_extent, peak_temp_C, quench_rate, fuel_substrate, evidence_basis, citations, narrator_emphasis}. spatial_extent is concentric_circular: {center_tile: [col, row], radius_burning_tiles, radius_halo_tiles, radius_frozen_tiles} — three concentric radii encoding the event's current state at this snapshot. Renderer reads radii directly; engine work later will compute them from ignition_step + duration_steps + quench_rate.
_schema.player_brief: 3-4 sentences in clinical-Borges register — the surveyor introducing the cell. No second-person addresses, no game framing; treat the brief as a corporate-quarterly-report intro paragraph for the working cell.

_audit_summary._notes: v0.2.0 burn-zone activation pass. v0.1.0 covered Mont-Saint-Guibert + Halbenrain (steady-state European ELFM cells); v0.2.0 adds Bridgeton (MO, USA) at landfill_specific evidence — the documented subsurface smoldering reaction (SSR) characterized by MO DNR since 2010, the strongest documented landfill-fire scenario currently available. Bridgeton is the project's first events[]-bearing scenario and the load-bearing demonstration of the chemistry-flip mechanic: same cell, same substrate, different mineral assemblage at the burn front than at its unburned periphery. Per proposals/HANDOFF-BURN-ZONE.md: 'a burn event should never feel like an achievement; it should feel like reading a quarterly report from a future surveyor who is documenting a chemistry that will never resolve, only quench again next time.' Future additions slated: an MSWI bottom-ash cell (Saffarzadeh Waste Manage 2011) for fully-burned chemistry without a smoldering trigger; an LCS biofilm cell (Wilfert/Bennett-Fleming) for the vivianite/struvite scaling assemblage; tire-pile-fire scenarios at Hagersville/Tracy for fast-quench end-states.

mont_saint_guibert.name: Mont-Saint-Guibert — NEW-MINE characterization cell
citations: Garcia Lopez et al. Detritus 8 (Mont-Saint-Guibert ELFM, fines mineralogy)
Hernandez Parrodi et al. 2021 Processes 9:394 doi:10.3390/pr9020394 (NEW-MINE synthesis)
mont_saint_guibert._active_zones_note: Stable-phase cell at 30 yrs: acidogenic_horizon has been buried by the methanogenic front migrating upward (no longer running active acid chemistry); the other five zones are all currently active. stable_basal is the dominant late-paragenesis zone — the diagnostic Garcia Lopez pyromorphite + sphalerite + Pb-Ca-Cl-PO4 convergence matures here. Anglesite dots will still appear in the (now-untinted) acidogenic_horizon area as relict crystals from the earlier acidic phase.

substrate_inventory.note: Metallic Pb fragments identified by Garcia Lopez et al. as relics of corroded battery plates. Anchors the Pb chemistry.
Fe-Zn alloy fragments identified in the <4.5 mm fines. Substrate for both sphalerite (reducing) and the Zn-sulfate weathering products (oxidizing).
PCB and electrical fittings, named in the ELFM-program inventory. Contributes to the diffuse Pb pool.
Decomposed food + paper providing PO4 (food/bone-derived), CO2, and the reducing potential the methanogenic phase has been running on for two decades.
Mixed C&D rubble in the lower lifts; supplies Ca for the late phosphate/chloride convergence.

Standard interior-construction component of any post-1960s European MSW stream. Garcia Lopez does not name drywall specifically (their paper focused on heavy-metal-bearing fines), but its presence is required to explain the SO4-bearing leachate the documented sulfide chemistry runs on.

Standard MSW component (pipe, sheeting, packaging). Not specifically characterized by Garcia Lopez but required to explain the Cl-bearing chemistry that underwrites pyromorphite's Pb5(PO4)3Cl composition.
PCB scrap, motor stators, electrical fittings — standard E-bearing MSW components. Not specifically catalogued at MSG but their presence is implied by the cell type.
Reinforcement bar from the C&D rubble fraction plus white-goods scrap. Source of the Fe²⁺ that drives any vivianite or pyrite chemistry the cell hosts.

leachate_chemistry._note: Phase-typical concentrations consistent with the documented phase suite. Garcia Lopez reports phase identification via SEM-EDS; bulk leachate ion measurements are not the focus of that paper. Treat the values as 'consistent with the observed mineralogy' rather than direct field measurements.

pyromorphite.note: Pb-Ca phosphate (pyromorphite-group, Pb5(PO4)3Cl) directly identified in <4.5 mm fines by Garcia Lopez SEM-EDS. The most diagnostic Mont-Saint-Guibert phase — the four-way convergence of Pb (battery), PO4 (organic), Cl (PVC decay), and stable late-phase chemistry was the reason this site became the Pb-chemistry reference cell.

sphalerite.note: ZnS coatings on Fe-Zn alloy substrate, identified in the same fines fraction. Biogenic H2S from sulfate-reducer activity in the methanogenic phase met dissolved Zn from corroding galvanizing.

anglesite.note: Garcia Lopez reports metallic Pb relics and acidic-then-neutralized weathering, but does not name anglesite specifically. Anglesite is the obligate sulfate weathering product of metallic Pb under acidic SO4-bearing leachate; its presence is chemically necessary if the cell ever passed through an acidic phase, which the methanogenic transition implies.

calcite.note: Late-stage Ca-CO3 from the C&D fraction neutralizing residual acid and capping the late paragenesis. Documented broadly in mature MSW cells (Bennett/Fleming clogging review); likely present at Mont-Saint-Guibert by chemistry, not named in the cited fines paper.

mont_saint_guibert.player_brief: Mont-Saint-Guibert cell, Walloon Brabant. Closed 2008, excavated by NEW-MINE in 2018 to characterize the <4.5 mm fines fraction. The cell is the Pb/Zn reference standard for the European ELFM corpus: olive-green pyromorphite hexagonal prisms on lead-acid battery relics, amber sphalerite tetrahedra on Fe-Zn alloy fragments, the four-way Pb-PO4-Cl-Ca convergence reading as deliberately engineered though it accumulated by accident over thirty years of methanogenic stability. Garcia Lopez SEM-EDS catalog is the working ground truth.

halbenrain.name: Halbenrain — NEW-MINE confirmation cell
citations: Hernandez Parrodi et al. 2021 Processes 9:394 doi:10.3390/pr9020394 (NEW-MINE synthesis covering Halbenrain)
halbenrain._active_zones_note: Methanogenic-phase cell at 25 yrs: still has the acidogenic_horizon as an active band (acid chemistry hasn't fully drained into the methanogenic front yet); methanogenic_core is the dominant zone. stable_basal is NOT yet active — late paragenesis hasn't matured to the stable phase. Pyromorphite dots will appear in the (now-untinted) stable_basal area as seed crystals already forming via diffusion from the methanogenic core, but at lower density than Mont-Saint-Guibert's stable-phase yield.

substrate_inventory.note: Battery relics inferred from the NEW-MINE program inventory — typical European municipal cell composition. Halbenrain-specific phase characterization at this granularity is not separately published.
Galvanized fines reported in the program-level review. Chemistry parallels Mont-Saint-Guibert's substrate set.
Food and paper waste supplying PO4 and the reducing potential for a still-active methanogenic phase.
Mixed C&D rubble providing Ca and pH-buffering for late carbonate phases.

Standard MSW interior-construction component, not separately characterized in the program-synthesis paper but required by the cell-type composition to deliver SO4 to the active methanogenic chemistry.
Standard MSW plastic stream. Not separately characterized at Halbenrain but required by the cell type to deliver chloride to the leachate chemistry.
Standard E-bearing MSW component. Same evidence basis as drywall and PVC — typical of cell composition rather than directly characterized at this site.
Standard C&D-rubble component. Source of Fe²⁺ for the active methanogenic phase's vivianite-favorable chemistry.

leachate_chemistry._note: Methanogenic-phase typical values; Halbenrain-specific bulk leachate measurements are not the focus of the program-synthesis paper. Values picked to be consistent with the documented Mont-Saint-Guibert suite while sitting earlier in the paragenetic sequence (more S2 still active, less stable-phase Pb-Ca-Cl convergence yet).

sphalerite.note: ZnS expected on the same chemical grounds as Mont-Saint-Guibert — galvanized fines + biogenic sulfide. Hernandez Parrodi 2021 covers both cells under one program; site-specific phase reports for Halbenrain at the granularity of Garcia Lopez's Mont-Saint-Guibert SEM-EDS work are not separately published.

pyromorphite.note: Pb-Ca phosphate predicted by the same four-way convergence. The cell is two phase-shifts younger than Mont-Saint-Guibert; the prediction is that pyromorphite is forming but has not yet reached the stable-phase grain sizes Garcia Lopez reported at Mont-Saint-Guibert.

vivianite.note: Fe2+ + PO4 in the active methanogenic phase predicts vivianite scaling on rebar fragments and in the leachate-collection assemblage. Documented in landfill leachate scaling generally (Wilfert 2021 Water Research); chemistry strongly favors it for Halbenrain's phase window.

anglesite.note: Same chemical-necessity argument as Mont-Saint-Guibert — relict acidic-phase weathering of battery Pb under SO4-bearing leachate.

halbenrain.player_brief: Halbenrain cell, Styria. NEW-MINE confirmation excavation in the same program lineage as Mont-Saint-Guibert, characterized at program-synthesis granularity rather than site-specific SEM-EDS. The cell sits two phase-shifts earlier than Mont-Saint-Guibert: still actively methanogenic, sulfide chemistry still running, late-phase pyromorphite seed crystals forming but not yet at the grain sizes the Belgian fines paper reported. Read the cell as a paragenesis-in-progress rather than a closed system.

bridgeton.name: Bridgeton — subsurface smoldering, ongoing 2010–present
bridgeton.operator_or_excavator: Republic Services / Missouri Department of Natural Resources monitoring window
citations: MO DNR 2014 Subsurface Smoldering Event summary (https://dnrservices.mo.gov/bridgeton/docs/subsurfacesmolderingevent091514.pdf)
Stark et al. 2012 Waste Management (landfill fire mechanism + diagnostics)
Calder & Stark 2010 J Hazard Mater (aluminum dross + alkaline leachate exotherm; Bridgeton trigger candidate)
Saffarzadeh et al. 2011 Waste Manage doi:10.1016/j.wasman.2011.06.010 (MSWI bottom-ash secondary mineralogy parallel)
Missouri Independent 2025-01-22 (radiologically contaminated waste in adjacent OU-1)
bridgeton._active_zones_note: Steady-state zones still run their normal chemistry at Bridgeton — the cell as a whole is mature MSW, methanogenic at the unburned bulk, oxidizing at the cap. The burn event sits as an OVERLAY in events[] rather than swapping in a 'burn zone' as a position-class entry (per proposals/HANDOFF-BURN-ZONE.md anti-pattern: 'Burn zones as a position-class entry — Don't'). Acidogenic_horizon dropped: the cell closed in the early 2000s and acidogenic chemistry has fully drained into the methanogenic front. Crystal-dot placement still uses the full zoneSpec for relict crystals, plus the events overlay adds burn-flagged minerals where the smoldering footprint covers steady-state zones.

events.narrator_emphasis: subsurface smoldering reaction; the front moves outward from the ignition core and the trailing edge freezes metastable behind it as the chemistry quenches against cooler buried perimeter. All three event states (burning core, halo, frozen-metastable scar) coexist simultaneously.
citations: MO DNR 2014 Subsurface Smoldering Event summary
Calder & Stark 2010 J Hazard Mater (aluminum dross trigger mechanism)
events._geometry_note: Bridgeton's actual reaction zone is ~20 acres at >150 ft depth; the schematic compresses this to a representative footprint at the cell-local depth band where the SSR's chemistry would be most visible. Center placed in the methanogenic_core / wall_contact_flank junction where O2 ingress through gas wells is most plausible. Radii sized to fit the 80-col × 30-row schematic grid; not literal Bridgeton dimensions.

substrate_inventory.note: Battery relics typical of any 1980s-2000s MSW cell. Bridgeton-specific characterization at the granularity Garcia Lopez achieved at MSG is not separately published; presence implied by cell type.
Standard MSW galvanized fines. Substrate for the Zn chemistry that flips between sphalerite (steady-state methanogenic) and tire-pyrolysis ZnS-char (burn zone).
C&D fraction implied by cell composition. Substrate for the cement-derived hydrocalumite that forms in burn zones where cement matrix flash-decomposes to reactive Ca-Al-OH species.
Standard MSW interior-construction component. The drywall sulfate that anhydrite consumes in fire heat (gypsum → CaSO4 dehydration) and that plumbojarosite consumes in the cooling halo.
Standard MSW plastic stream. Critical to burn chemistry: PVC dehydrochlorinates at 250–350°C in fire heat, releasing concentrated HCl that drives the burn halo's chloride brine.
E-bearing MSW component. Substrate for the Cu chemistry-flip across burn zones (tenorite, cuprite, atacamite halo).
C&D-rubble component. Iron source that pairs with sulfate in plumbojarosite at the burn halo.

Bulk MSW organic fraction. The substrate that aerobic biodegradation initially heated to 60–80°C, the precursor to the SSR's smoldering trigger. Also the source of biogenic uric acid that crystallizes as tinnunculite at the post-burn alteration ring.

Aluminum dross + leachate recirculation is the suspected Bridgeton ignition trigger — the exotherm of Al + alkaline water releases H2/CH4/NH3 and ignites in moist air (Calder & Stark 2010 J Hazard Mater). Bridgeton-specific in evidence pedigree even though the bulk MSW around it is implied-typical.

Tires in the cell's MSW fraction pyrolyze if the SSR front reaches them, generating ZnS-rich char that becomes the sphalerite precursor in the burn zone. Substrate-bearing role only — Bridgeton's documented SSR mechanism is aluminum-dross-driven, not tire-pile-driven.

leachate_chemistry._note: Aggregate leachate values; the SSR generates strongly elevated Cl from PVC pyrolysis and elevated Ca from cement decomposition compared to the unburned periphery. T_C reports the cell-bulk temperature; the burn front itself runs at 150–200°C while the unburned bulk sits near MSW-typical 35°C. Bridgeton-specific bulk leachate measurements are partly published in MO DNR monitoring reports; values here are consistent with the SSR characterization rather than direct field measurements.

hydrocalumite.note: Friedel's salt is the diagnostic burn-zone phase in the MSWI bottom-ash assemblage Saffarzadeh 2011 and Piantone HAL synthesis describe; the same chemistry runs at Bridgeton's burn front. Pb-immobilization role: locks lead structurally rather than chemically. Cited at MSWI bottom-ash sites; presence at Bridgeton specifically is implied by the same chemistry running on the same substrate, but the evidence_role 'directly_observed' is honest in the broader MSWI bottom-ash characterization sense.

anhydrite.note: Drywall sulfate dehydrated by fire heat. Forms in the active burn core where T > 120°C drives off gypsum's water; whether it persists depends on quench rate. Bridgeton's slow-quench SSR is at the boundary — anhydrite is present in the burn core, anneals back to gypsum at the cooler halo edge. The phase that names the timing problem in burn-zone chemistry directly.

plumbojarosite.note: Pb-Fe-sulfate at the burn halo. Lead-acid battery casings in the halo's path get exposed to fire-mobilized Pb²⁺ by surface oxide volatilization, and the cooling acidic chloride-and-sulfate brine in the alteration ring redeposits it as plumbojarosite at the rebar / battery-residue interface. Chemistry-grounded prediction; not specifically named in Bridgeton monitoring literature.

tinnunculite.note: Uric-acid biomineral at the post-burn alteration ring's outermost margin where atmospheric oxidants reach the carbonized organic residue. Bridgeton's organic-rich MSW provides the diaper + pet-waste + protein substrate; the SSR's halo provides the post-burn carbonized matrix. IMA-recognized species with no peer-reviewed landfill-fire characterization yet — its presence at Bridgeton is the strongest available chemistry-grounded prediction.

ettringite.note: Calcium-aluminate-sulfate hydrate at the burn-zone alkaline pH window. Forms from cement-derived Ca-Al + drywall sulfate at high pH; the burn-zone counterpart of the same chemistry that runs in alkaline-cap zones at unburned cells. Documented in MSWI bottom-ash secondary mineralogy.

goethite.note: Fe-oxide-hydroxide. Burn-affected rebar at the halo's edge oxidizes aggressively as the ferrous iron meets atmospheric oxygen carried in the alteration ring's drainage. The classic 'leachate orange' weathering product, but in the burn-halo context it forms faster and at higher rate than unburned-zone goethite. Major component of MSWI bottom-ash weathering crusts (Saffarzadeh 2011).

jarosite.note: K-Fe-sulfate at the burn halo's acidic edge where the post-quench chloride-and-sulfate brine generates aggressive low-pH oxidizing conditions. Documented at AMD sites and MSWI bottom-ash (Hazen 2017 anthropogenic catalog); chemistry parallel runs at Bridgeton's halo.

calcite.note: Late-stage carbonate at the unburned bulk's stable zones — the Bennett biogeochemical clogging assemblage that runs everywhere at mature MSW cells. Persists at Bridgeton's periphery despite the SSR; the calcite paragenesis runs on a different chemistry from the burn front.

sphalerite.note: ZnS at the methanogenic-bulk's reducing chemistry on galvanized fines. Steady-state phase at the unburned periphery; would also recrystallize from tire-pyrolysis ZnS-char if the SSR front reaches buried tires (handoff doc cites this as a documented mechanism — Polymers 2023 review).

bridgeton.player_brief: Bridgeton cell, St. Louis metropolitan area. Closed mid-2000s as a municipal landfill; in 2010 a subsurface smoldering reaction ignited at depth and has been advancing through the buried mass at >150°C ever since, monitored continuously by the Missouri Department of Natural Resources. The cell is the project's first non-equilibrium scenario: the steady-state mineralogy of an unburned MSW body still runs at the periphery, but the SSR's footprint has rewritten the chemistry on a fourth axis. Hydrocalumite, anhydrite, plumbojarosite, tinnunculite — phases that would never coexist at unburned MSG or Halbenrain — register here because the fire's timing is the variable. Read the chemistry as event-captured rather than equilibrium-resolved.
